1: Psalm 47 is an enthronement psalm — a psalm celebrating YHWH's kingship over all nations. The enthronement psalms (Ps 47, 93, 96-99) were likely used in the autumn festival (Sukkot/Tabernacles) and in Jewish tradition Ps 47 is read seven times before the blowing of the shofar on Rosh Hashanah (the Jewish New Year), celebrating YHWH's enthronement as cosmic king. 'Clap your hands, all you peoples; shout to God with a joyful cry' — the hand-clapping (tiq'u kaf) and shouting (teruah) were the formal acclamations performed at a king's coronation in the ancient Near East. All peoples (kol-ha'amim) — not just Israel — are summoned to perform this royal acclamation.
2: 'For the LORD Most High is to be feared; he is a great King over the whole earth' — 'LORD Most High' (Yhwh Elyon) — combining YHWH's covenant name with the Canaanite title El Elyon (God Most High, the supreme deity of the Canaanite pantheon; cf. Gen 14:18-20: Melchizedek priest of El Elyon; Deut 32:8: El Elyon dividing the nations). The Psalm appropriates the supreme Canaanite divine title and applies it to YHWH: YHWH is not one deity among many but the supreme king over the entire earth. 'Great King over the whole earth' (melech gadol al-kol-ha'arets) — the universal scope that the psalm will elaborate in vv. 7-9.
3: 'He subdues peoples under us and nations beneath our feet' — the covenant warfare of YHWH on Israel's behalf: YHWH as the divine warrior who gives his client-people victory over nations. 'Under us' (tachtenu) and 'beneath our feet' (tachat raglenu) are the postures of the defeated enemy in the ancient Near East — the image of the king standing on the necks of prostrate enemies was a standard motif in Egyptian and Mesopotamian royal iconography. The footstool image of Ps 110:1 ('until I make your enemies your footstool') uses the same imagery.
4: 'He chose our inheritance for us, the glory of Jacob whom he loved. Selah' — YHWH's choice (yivchar) of Israel's inheritance (nachalatenu, our inheritance/portion = the land of Canaan and the covenant relationship) is the foundation of all the military and political celebration. Behind the nations' subjection and Israel's ascendancy lies not military prowess but divine election and love. 'The glory of Jacob' (ge'on ya'aqov, the pride/majesty of Jacob) is the land or the status that YHWH gives to the covenant people. Selah again marks the liturgical pause for the congregation to receive this theological claim before the second strophe of praise (vv. 5-9).
5: 'God has ascended with shouts of joy, the LORD with the sound of a ram's horn (shofar)' — the ascent (alah Elohim) is the key verse for the psalm's function as an enthronement song. The 'ascent' refers to YHWH ascending to his throne — probably enacted in liturgical procession where the ark (the throne of YHWH's presence) was carried to the sanctuary amid shouts of joy (teruah) and the shofar blast. The same event is narrated in 2 Sam 6:15 (ark brought to Jerusalem with 'shouting and the sound of trumpets'). The Psalm dramatizes YHWH's royal ascent to the heavenly court.
6: 'Sing praises to God, sing praises! Sing praises to our King, sing praises!' — the fourfold call to praise (zammeru Elohim zammeru) is a liturgical intensification: the same word repeated four times in four successive half-verses. Zamar (sing praises, make music) is the term for the musical praise of the temple liturgy (Ps 9:2; 21:13; 30:4; 57:7-9; 68:4, 32). The repetition creates the effect of cumulative acclamation — a liturgical crescendo of communal praise. 'Our King' (malchenu) moves from the cosmic scope ('God') to the covenant relationship ('our').
7: 'For God is the King of the whole earth; sing praises with understanding (maskil)' — 'maskil' here (usually a term in psalm superscriptions) appears in the body of the psalm, meaning 'with wisdom/discernment' — worship that understands what it is doing and why. The 'whole earth' (kol-ha'arets) as YHWH's kingdom: not merely Israel, not merely the ancient Near East, but the entire inhabited world. The commanded wisdom in praising ensures that the worship is not mere emotional enthusiasm but intelligent acknowledgment of what is true about YHWH's sovereignty.
8: 'God reigns over the nations; God is seated on his holy throne' — the present-tense declaration: YHWH reigns (malach Elohim al-goyim) now, actively, over nations. The 'holy throne' (kissei qodsho) is both the ark of the covenant as the earthly throne and the cosmic throne above the heavens (Isa 6:1: 'I saw the Lord seated on a high and lofty throne'). The enthronement is not merely anticipated (future) but affirmed (present): YHWH's kingship over the nations is a current reality, not only an eschatological hope.
9: 'The nobles of the nations have gathered as the people of the God of Abraham; for the rulers of the earth belong to God; he is greatly exalted' — the eschatological ingathering: the 'nobles' (nedivim) or princes of the nations are gathered as the people of the God of Abraham. This is the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:3: 'all peoples of the earth shall be blessed through you'): the nations' leaders join the covenant community of YHWH's people. 'For the rulers of the earth belong to God' (ki le-Elohim maginei-arets, for the shields of the earth belong to God) — all national power and authority is ultimately subject to the divine king. The psalm closes with the maximum scope: YHWH greatly exalted above all earthly sovereignty.
